heb.paragraph: The name David shares this root. Dōd is intimate love — the word for the lover throughout the poem. It carries warmth, physical desire, and committed affection.

"My nard sends forth its fragrance" (v.12). Nard is an expensive imported perfume from the Himalayas. The same perfume Mary of Bethany pours on Jesus’ feet (John 12:3). Love and extravagance are inseparable.

panels.ctx: The poem opens with the woman’s voice — unusual in ancient literature. She desires her lover’s kisses, praises his name as poured-out perfume, and asks him to take her away. The Daughters of Jerusalem serve as a chorus. She describes herself as "dark but lovely" (v.5) — sun-darkened from vineyard labour, not pampered. Her beauty is working beauty.

cross.note: Mary anoints Jesus with nard — the same perfume, the same extravagance of love.
Paul reads marital love as a picture of Christ and the church — the allegorical tradition rooted in Song.

notes.note: "Solomon’s Song of Songs" — the superlative: the greatest song. As "Holy of Holies" means the holiest place, "Song of Songs" means the supreme song.
"Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth" — the woman speaks first. Her desire is uninhibited. "Your love is more delightful than wine" — love intoxicates more than the finest vintage.
"Dark am I, yet lovely." Sun-darkened from vineyard work. She apologises for her appearance but asserts her beauty. Insecurity and confidence in the same breath.
She asks where he grazes his flock — she wants to find him. The Daughters reply: follow the tracks. Love seeks and finds.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "your love is better than wine" — love and wine are the poem’s twin metaphors for intoxication. Both alter perception; both produce joy.
Alter: "dark am I, yet lovely" — the conjunction can also be translated "and," making it affirmation rather than concession: dark AND lovely.

Calvin: the Song celebrates marriage as God’s gift. The church fathers read it allegorically (Christ and the church); Calvin affirms both the literal (marriage) and the spiritual (divine love) readings.
Calvin: the woman’s desire for her beloved mirrors the soul’s desire for God. "Let him kiss me" — the longing for intimate presence, not just knowledge about.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "Song of Songs" is a superlative construction (like "King of Kings" or "Holy of Holies"). This is the greatest song.
The NET discusses the kedar/Solomon tent wordplay: "dark like the tents of Kedar, like the tent curtains of Solomon" — both are dark but the second is luxurious.
Longman: the woman’s initiative is remarkable in the ANE context. She is not passive; she pursues. The Song grants women full agency in romantic love.
Longman: "dark but lovely" addresses social prejudice — dark skin from outdoor labour marked lower class. The Beloved reclaims her beauty against the standard.
Garrett: the title attributes the poem to Solomon but may mean "about Solomon" or "in the Solomonic tradition." The royal setting provides the poem’s backdrop.
Garrett: the opening establishes the dramatic situation: the Beloved longs for the Lover, seeks him, and the Daughters of Jerusalem witness. The poem will alternate voices throughout.

sections.header: Verses 9–17 — “How Beautiful You Are, My Darling”

heb.gloss: my darling / my companion / my friend
heb.paragraph: "How beautiful you are, my darling (raʿyātî)!" (v.15). Raʿyâ means companion, friend, intimate associate. The lover does not just desire the beloved; he befriends her. The relationship is erotic AND companionate.

panels.ctx: The Lover speaks: she is beautiful as a mare among Pharaoh’s chariots (v.9) — a striking image of decorated beauty and controlled power. Her cheeks are adorned, her neck circled. He will make her earrings of gold and silver (vv.10–11). She responds: his love is fragrant as myrrh between her breasts (v.13), as henna blossoms in En Gedi (v.14). The dialogue closes with mutual admiration: you are beautiful; our bed is verdant; our house has cedar beams (vv.15–17).

cross.note: "A wife of noble character... her husband has full confidence in her" — the marital partnership the Song dramatises.
"The wedding of the Lamb has come" — the eschatological marriage feast that the Song foreshadows.

notes.note: "I liken you to a mare among Pharaoh’s chariot horses." Egyptian chariot horses were magnificent — adorned, powerful, bred for beauty. The comparison is of ornamented splendour.
"While the king was at his table, my perfume spread its fragrance. My beloved is to me a sachet of myrrh resting between my breasts." The fragrance imagery: love is sensory, bodily, aromatic.
"How beautiful you are, my darling! Your eyes are doves. How handsome you are, my beloved! Our bed is verdant." Mutual admiration. Both voices speak. The relationship is reciprocal.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: myrrh between the breasts and henna in En Gedi — the fragrance imagery is both erotic and geographic. En Gedi is a desert oasis: love blooms in unexpected places.
Alter: "our bed is verdant" — the outdoors as bedroom. Nature participates in the lovers’ joy. The Song’s ecology is not backdrop but participant.
Calvin: "your eyes are doves" begins a pattern of nature-metaphors for the beloved’s body. Calvin: the comparisons honour the body as God’s creation. Physical beauty is praised, not apologised for.
Calvin: the shared bed under cedar beams is marriage’s legitimate intimacy. Calvin reads the Song as a defence of marital sexuality against both asceticism and licentiousness.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: Pharaoh’s chariot horses were legendary for their beauty and adornment — golden bridles, jewelled trappings. The comparison is to adorned magnificence.
The NET: "sachet of myrrh resting between my breasts" is intimate imagery. Myrrh was worn as a perfume pouch. The beloved carries her lover’s fragrance on her body.
Longman: the mare image is about decorated beauty, not speed or wildness. Pharaoh’s chariot horses were the most lavishly adorned animals in the ANE.
Longman: the bed-and-house imagery shifts from court to pastoral: green bed, cedar beams, fir rafters. Nature is their bedroom. The Song moves freely between urban and rural settings.
Garrett: the comparison to Pharaoh’s horses is a royal compliment: you are as stunning as the finest things in the most powerful kingdom on earth.
Garrett: the mutual dialogue establishes the Song’s pattern: she speaks, he speaks, they speak together. The voices interweave rather than alternate rigidly.

ppl.text: Beloved is a key figure in The Beloved’s Longing.

No wonder the young women love you!ESVyour anointing oils are fragrant; your name is oil poured out; therefore virgins love you.NIVTell me, you whom I love, where you graze your flock and where you rest your sheep at midday. Why should I be like a veiled woman beside the flocks of your friends?ESVTell me, you whom my soul loves, where you pasture your flock, where you make it lie down at noon; for why should I be like one who veils herself beside the flocks of your companions? rec.text: ""Solomon’s Song of Songs" — the superlative: the greatest song. As "Holy of Holies" means the holiest place, "Song of Songs" means the supreme song."

rows.label: Mutual Admiration — 1:9–17
rows.text: Mare among chariots. Myrrh between breasts. "How beautiful you are." Reciprocal desire.

"My nard sends forth its fragrance" (v.12). Nard is an expensive imported perfume from the Himalayas. The same perfume Mary of Bethany pours on Jesus’ feet (John 12:3). Love and extravagance are inseparable.

themes.note: Covenant peaks: love as commitment. Prophecy: the allegorical tradition reads marital love as Christ/church. Mercy: love as gift, not transaction.
